moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim to the central e5 and c5 squares while opening lines for our dark-squared bishop and queen. This move launches us into the rich world of closed openings, where strategic maneuvering and pawn structure often matter more than quick tactical shots. The d4 opening tends to lead to slower, more positional games compared to 1.e4, giving us excellent chances to build a solid position and gradually improve our pieces. Our main plan revolves around controlling the center and preparing c4 to challenge Black's central presence, which is the hallmark of the Queen's Gambit complex.

Black responds symmetrically in the center, immediately contesting our control of the e4 and c4 squares while establishing their own central foothold. This classical response shows that our opponent wants to fight for central space rather than allow us to dominate with pawns on d4 and e4. The d5 pawn also prepares natural development of the dark-squared bishop to f5 or g4, and supports potential pawn breaks like ...c5 or ...e6. We should expect Black to continue developing pieces harmoniously while maintaining this central tension.

We're launching the famous Queen's Gambit, offering our c-pawn to deflect Black's central d5 pawn and potentially gain a lead in central control. This isn't a true gambit since we'll usually regain the pawn easily, but it puts immediate pressure on Black's position and forces them to make an important decision about the center. If Black captures with dxc4, we can recapture and gain time developing our pieces, while if they maintain the tension or decline with moves like e6, we get a different but equally rich middlegame. The Queen's Gambit is one of the most solid and reliable openings at all levels, giving us excellent practical chances.

Black strikes back in the center with a counter-gambit of their own, immediately challenging our d4 pawn and refusing to be pushed around. This aggressive response is characteristic of the Austrian Attack system, where Black seeks active piece play and central counterplay rather than passive defense. Our opponent is essentially saying 'if you're going to attack my d5 pawn, I'll attack your d4 pawn right back!' This creates immediate tactical complications and sharp play, quite different from the more positional lines that arise after moves like 2...e6 or 2...dxc4. We need to decide whether to capture on c5 or d5, and each choice leads to completely different pawn structures and plans.

We're accepting Black's challenge and capturing the c5 pawn, which gives us an extra pawn in the center but also allows Black's d-pawn to advance with tempo. This is the most principled continuation, as we're following the general rule of capturing towards the center when possible. By taking on c5, we open the d-file for our queen and potentially our rook later, while also removing Black's central pawn that was attacking our d4 square. However, we must be prepared for Black's likely continuation with d4, which will advance with great force and create immediate threats in our position.

Black unleashes the Salvio Countergambit, pushing their d-pawn deep into our territory with tremendous force and creating immediate tactical threats. This aggressive pawn advance attacks our c5 pawn, forces our queen to move from d1, and gives Black excellent piece activity in return for the sacrificed pawn. The d4 pawn also cramps our position significantly, making it harder for us to develop our pieces naturally while giving Black targets to attack. We're now in sharp tactical territory where both sides will need to calculate carefully - Black has dynamic compensation for the pawn in the form of piece activity and attacking chances, while we have the material advantage but must navigate carefully to consolidate our extra pawn without falling behind in development.
